title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the Eiffel Tower. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Iron Lady: From Eyesore to Icon
content.text: When the Eiffel Tower was first proposed for the 1889 World's Fair, it was met with a wave of public outrage. A group of Paris’s most prominent artists and writers signed a manifesto calling it a 'gigantic black smokestack' that would disfigure the city’s elegant skyline. They could not have imagined that this temporary structure would become the most visited paid monument in the world. ||(41)|| [41]Gustave Eiffel, the engineer behind the project, defended his creation by arguing that it was a triumph of modern science[/41]. He believed that the beauty of a structure lay in its utility and its mathematical precision. Despite the protests, the tower was completed in just over two years, standing as the tallest man-made structure on Earth for the next four decades. ||(42)|| [42]This immense height was made possible by the use of puddled iron, a material that was lighter and stronger than stone[/42]. The tower’s lattice design was not just aesthetic; it was a practical solution to the problem of wind resistance. By allowing the air to pass through the structure, Eiffel ensured that his tower would remain stable even during the fiercest storms. ||(43)|| [43]Despite its popularity during the fair, the tower was originally intended to be demolished after twenty years[/43]. The city of Paris had only granted Eiffel a temporary lease on the land, and many still viewed the iron giant as a cluttering presence on the Champ de Mars. ||(44)|| [44]To prove its worth, Eiffel began installing scientific instruments on the tower, including a weather station and a physical laboratory[/44]. However, it was the birth of radio that truly saved the monument. The tower’s height made it an ideal location for a wireless telegraphy transmitter, which proved vital for military communications during the First World War. ||(45)|| [45]This strategic importance changed the public’s perception of the tower[/45]. It was no longer just a piece of exhibition architecture; it was a vital part of the nation’s infrastructure. The demolition plans were permanently shelved, and the 'Iron Lady' began her journey toward becoming a global symbol of France. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the tower is repainted every seven years to prevent rust, a process that requires sixty tons of paint and 18 months of labor[/46]. It remains a testament to the idea that great architecture often requires the courage to challenge the tastes of the time. The smokestack that everyone hated has become the icon that everyone loves.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] the 'Committee of Three Hundred' [/41]—one member for each meter of the tower's height—was fierce in its opposition. They feared that the sheer industrial nature of the iron beams would overwhelm the classical beauty of Paris, which was then defined by stone and marble.
paragraphs.B: At [42] 300 meters tall [/42], it dwarfed every other building in the city. The sheer scale of the project required over 18,000 individual iron parts and 2.5 million rivets, all of which had to be designed with millimetric accuracy to ensure the structure’s integrity.
paragraphs.C: This [43] engineering brilliance [/43] was matched by its immediate commercial success. During the 1889 Exposition, nearly two million people paid to climb to the top, marvelling at the unprecedented views of Paris and proving that there was a deep public fascination with the machine age.
paragraphs.D: As [44] the expiration date of the lease approached [/44], Eiffel realized he needed to find a permanent function for the tower if he wanted to save it from the wrecking ball. He knew that the only way to justify its existence was to make it indispensable to the state.
paragraphs.E: The [45] success of these early transmissions [/45] demonstrated that the tower had a value far beyond its appearance. By the 1920s, it was broadcasting the first public radio programs in Europe, further cementing its place in the modern life of the city.
paragraphs.F: This [46] transition into a cultural icon [/46] was finalized after the Second World War. As tourism exploded in the 1950s, the tower became a must-see destination for travelers from across the globe, synonymous with the romance and history of the French capital.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] other engineers at the time [/47] were skeptical of Eiffel's design. They argued that such a tall structure would be unstable and that the sheer weight of the iron would cause it to sink into the soft soil of the riverbank, a prediction that fortunately proved false.

questions.explanation: A refers to the 'Committee of Three Hundred' and their opposition, linking back to the 'manifesto' and 'public outrage' mentioned in the introduction.
B mentions the tower's height (300 meters) and its status as the 'tallest man-made structure' described in the preceding text.
C refers to the 'engineering brilliance' (the wind resistance solution) and introduces the 'commercial success' during the 1889 fair.
D connects the 'expiration date' of the temporary lease mentioned in the text to Eiffel's plan to make the tower 'indispensable'.
E links the 'early transmissions' to the radio technology and military communications described just before the gap.
F refers to the 'transition into a cultural icon' and explains how tourism after WWII solidified the tower's status as a global symbol.
